content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Because early selection is codified as a narrow, separately-justified legal exception rather than the structural default of secondary education, reform of the selection age is legally more tractable than reshaping Hungary's broader maintainer or curriculum architecture — but legitimacy and constitutional doctrine require any change to proceed on a felmenő-rendszer (grade-by-grade phase-in) basis with minimum ~1-year statutory lead time, protecting students already admitted under existing rules.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Hungarian law explicitly allows gymnasium (4, 6 or 8-grade forms) but treats the 6/8-year track as a distinct legal category requiring 'talent-nurturing' (tehetséggondozás) justification with separately regulated elevated standards, making selection age a codified exception rather than the structural default.

The Nkt. § 4 defines secondary education (középfokú nevelés-oktatás szakasza) as beginning at grade 9, with the 6- and 8-year gymnasium explicitly listed as the only statutory exception to this age boundary.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: Whether Hungary's Constitutional Court (Alkotmánybíróság) would treat abolition or significant raising of the 6/8-year selection age as a matter binding the felmenő-rendszer safeguard as mandatory constitutional doctrine, or whether it would classify it as a discretionary legislative choice subject only to rule-of-law process review.

Whether non-state maintainers (churches, foundations under remaining autonomy frameworks) would face differential legal constraints or political veto power in a national selection-age reform, and whether consensus-building with maintainer federations would constitute a hard prerequisite or a discretionary negotiation.
